cataloging_subject.label: The Romans of Partenay
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the anonymous Middle English rhyme-royal translation and adaptation of Coudrette's French Roman de Melusine or Roman de Parthenay. It is distinct from Coudrette's source work, the unique surviving English witness Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.17, Cambridge University Library MS Ll.2.5 as Skeat's French comparison manuscript, Skeat's 1866 and revised 1899 editions, the University of Michigan encoding, the repository XML, and their digital reproductions.

titles.value: The Romans of Partenay, or of Lusignen

titles.supplied_by: Skeat's edition subtitle and modern cataloging
notes: This widely used discovery title does not identify the Middle English work with Jean d'Arras's distinct prose Melusine.

titles.value: The Romance of Earl Raymond of Poitiers in English
notes: This is a manuscript-catalog alternative title, not an authorial title.

titles.value: For full fayne I wold do that myght you please
notes: This is the surviving incipit on f.2r; folio 1 and therefore the original opening are wanting.
The manuscript and edition end with this rubric after Middle English line 6615.

agents.name: Coudrette
notes: Author of the French verse source completed in 1401; not the anonymous Middle English translator.

agents.name: Walter W. Skeat
notes: Editor of the 1866 text and its revised 1899 reprint; not the medieval translator.

agents.name: Jenni Nuttall
notes: Author of the 2016 peer-reviewed reassessment of the translator, date context, and possible patronage.

scope.description: Responsibility for the Middle English expression cataloged as CME00045

notes: This author statement names the anonymous creator of the Middle English translation-adaptation; Coudrette's authorship belongs to the distinct French source work.

scope.description: Authorship of the French verse source work translated into Middle English

notes: Source-work authorship is recorded separately from responsibility for the Middle English expression.

The translator says that French was not his native language and discusses the demands of rendering Coudrette's octosyllabic French into English rhyme royal. His name is not preserved because the opening leaf is lost.

scope.description: Formal and lexical adaptation into seven-line English rhyme-royal stanzas

notes: The epilogue describes near line-by-line translation but also word-order changes and compression or expansion required by metre.

notes: Skeat states that proof sheets were twice compared with the manuscript; the edition still contains stated punctuation, expansion, typography, and supply interventions.

notes: This edition-layer translation is not part of the medieval translator's surviving Middle English text.

assertion.notes: The Database of Middle English Romance gives c. 1500. Nuttall places the poem's language and patronage possibilities in a mid-fifteenth-century context but does not establish an alternative exact date; no numeric range is normalized.

date_statements.display: Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.17, late fifteenth or around 1500
notes: The witness date is not automatically the translation date; the current Trinity catalog gives the fifteenth century and DMeR gives c. 1500.

places.label: Northeast Midlands, England
notes: A question-mark localization in the Database of Middle English Romance; not a secure composition place.

places.label: Poitou
notes: The principal setting and dynastic geography of the Lusignan and Parthenay story; not accepted here as the English translation's place of composition.

assertion.notes: The source itself prints a question mark. This is not converted into a firm authorial location and remains distinct from Trinity R.3.17's copying history.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The XML's 57 French and 57 modern English editorial supply lines represent Skeat's replacement for missing folio 88 and do not make the medieval work trilingual.

form_statements.description: Narrative verse in seven-line rhyme-royal stanzas, normally decasyllabic and rhyming ababbcc; Skeat's prose-like modern English line rendering of the missing folio is editorial matter.

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English rhyme-royal translation and adaptation of Coudrette's French verse romance. The fairy Melusine marries Raymond of Poitiers on condition that he never seeks to see her on Saturdays, builds his power and dynasty, and bears sons whose adventures extend the Lusignan lineage across Europe and the eastern Mediterranean. Raymond's breach of secrecy exposes Melusine's serpentine transformation and separates the couple; later quests, combats, family catastrophes, dynastic praise, prayer, and the translator's reflections on poetic translation bring the poem to its close.

The work survives uniquely in Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.17. Folio 1, containing the original opening and perhaps information about the anonymous translator and patron, is lost. Folio 88 is also lost; Skeat replaces its 56-line Middle English interval with 57 French lines from CUL MS Ll.2.5 and a 57-line modern English rendering. These editorial supplies are represented in the XML but are not surviving Middle English text.

extent.description: The XML contains 6,769 surviving Middle English L elements in 967 rhyme-royal LG elements: a 210-line surviving prologue and 6,559 extant lines in the separately numbered poem. The poem's lineation runs 1–6,615, but lines 4,551–4,606 are absent with folio 88; canonical lineation therefore exceeds surviving Middle English by 56 lines. Skeat inserts 57 French L elements and 57 modern English L elements for that gap, producing a raw XML total of 6,883 L and 969 LG. The XML has 229 PB elements but only 227 unique REF values: one title-page image plus 226 numbered edition pages, with pp. 158–159 duplicated across the parallel editorial supply streams. It also has 1,877 marginal and 343 footnote NOTE elements.

content_parts.label: Surviving translator's prologue
content_parts.summary: The translator reflects on French, metre, prior authorities, pagan invocation, patronage, and his intention to translate Coudrette's romance.

locators.value: begins f.2r; preceding f.1 is wanting

content_parts.label: Translated romance narrative
content_parts.summary: The main Melusine and Lusignan narrative through the point where Skeat notes that the Cambridge copy of the French romance ends.

locators.value: f.5v through f.121v, with f.88 wanting
French supply begins f.103v for the R.3.17 f.88 gap

notes: The 6,377 metric counts surviving Middle English lines only. The numbered range contains the 56-line physical lacuna, represented editorially by 57 French and 57 modern English lines.

content_parts.label: Concluding litany
content_parts.summary: A prayer for the line of Partenay invoking the Trinity, Virgin, angels, apostles, martyrs, confessors, and saints.

content_parts.label: Translator's epilogue
content_parts.summary: Ten stanzas describing the translator's fidelity, rearrangement of words for metre, relation of French and English line lengths, and appeal for the patron's indulgence.

related_works.title: Roman de Melusine (Roman de Parthenay or Roman de Lusignan)
notes: Coudrette completed the Middle French verse romance in 1401. Translation_of records the textual source relation without identifying CUL Ll.2.5 as the anonymous English translator's physical exemplar.

related_works.title: Liber Proverbiorum
notes: Nuttall argues that shared diction and translation theory make common translators highly likely, but identity and the resulting dating or patronage implications remain hypotheses rather than settled responsibility.

catalog_summary.title: The Romans of Partenay
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator
catalog_summary.date: Probably c. 1500; unique witness late fifteenth century or c. 1500

notes: Coudrette is source author, not author of the Middle English translation. The University of Michigan item inherits a generic author heading for Couldrette that must not erase the anonymous translator.

Nuttall's common-translator argument linking this poem with Liber Proverbiorum is recorded as a supported hypothesis. Margaret of Anjou is a plausible but unproved patron because the identifying opening leaf is lost.

Skeat's outdated suggestion that the translator might have been born of English parents in Poitou is not promoted into a place-of-composition assertion; the current DMeR question-mark localization is retained with low confidence.

The complete Trinity R.3.17 digital surrogate is a reproduction layer. Its CC BY-NC image license and Trinity's publication-permission policy do not govern the underlying fifteenth-century text or authorize commercial/POD reuse of the images.

open_questions.question: Who translated The Romans of Partenay, when was it translated, was the same person responsible for Liber Proverbiorum, and was Margaret of Anjou or another noble patron the commissioner?
next_steps: Review current textual, linguistic, codicological, and patronage scholarship after Nuttall 2016.
Keep the c. 1500 catalog date, mid-fifteenth-century contextual argument, common-translator hypothesis, and Margaret attribution as separate claims.

open_questions.question: Can the English translation's dialect or place of composition be localized more securely than DMeR's 'Northeast Midlands?' without transferring the surviving scribe's language to the translator?
next_steps: Consult current LALME/eLALME, rhyme, and scribal-language studies and distinguish authorial from witness localization.

open_questions.question: How do Skeat's 57 French and 57 modern English editorial lines map reading by reading onto the 56 absent Middle English line numbers 4551–4606?
next_steps: Obtain a complete reproduction of CUL Ll.2.5 and collate f.103v and following with Skeat pp. 158–159.
Do not number Skeat's editorial supply as surviving Middle English lines.

evidence.locator: IDG, title page, BODY/DIV1, four DIV2 units, two supply DIV3 streams, LG, L, PB, MILESTONE, NOTE, and final TRAILER
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for repository identity, preferred and alternative titles, surviving incipit and explicit, Middle English verse, narrative and translation themes, part boundaries, lacuna representation, lineation, editorial languages, and structural counts.

evidence.locator: ancestor- and language-aware counts of DIV, LG, L, PB, MILESTONE, and NOTE
evidence.summary: Confirmed 6,769 Middle English lines in 967 stanza groups, 57 French and 57 modern English supply lines in two additional groups, 6,883 raw L, 969 LG, 229 PB but 227 unique image references, and 2,220 notes split into 1,877 marginal and 343 footnotes.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English, CME00045 item record
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage evidence for the stable item identity, title, Couldrette source heading, 1866/1899 edition statement, public digital text, and U-M rights assertion.

evidence.citation: Walter W. Skeat, ed., The Romans of Partenay, revised and reprinted 1899
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage evidence for edition title and date, R.3.17 and Ll.2.5, manuscript losses, source and comparison method, stanza and transcription practices, editorial supply, plot, linguistic observations, printed lineation, and the 1899 revision layer.

evidence.citation: Walter W. Skeat, ed., The Romans of Partenay (London, 1866)
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage evidence for the first edition, its original publication date, title, pagination, and relation to the revised 1899 reprint.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge, James Catalogue, MS R.3.17
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage evidence for the unique witness's shelfmark, catalog titles, fifteenth-century date, paper support, 123-folio extent, four-stanza page format, absent folios 1 and 88, imperfect incipit, f.124v explicit, provenance, and complete IIIF route.

evidence.citation: Biblissima, Cambridge University Library MS Ll.2.5
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage evidence for the French comparison manuscript, its fifteenth-century date, Coudrette romance on fols. 1–148, and following French chronicle on fols. 149r–161v.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, 'Romans of Partenay'
evidence.summary: Current specialist record for the work title, c. 1500 date, questioned Northeast Midlands localization, rhyme-royal form, plot and motifs, unique R.3.17 witness at ff. 2r–124v, 6,615-line main-poem extent, and derivation from Coudrette's romance.

Specialist authority record identifying Coudrette, the French romance's completion in 1401, Middle French language, romance genre, mainly octosyllabic rhyming-couplet form, and the Middle English Romans of Partenay as a translation.

evidence.citation: Jenni Nuttall, 'Margaret of Anjou as Patron of English Verse?: The Liber Proverbiorum and the Romans of Partenay', Review of English Studies 67 (2016), 636–659
evidence.summary: Peer-reviewed evidence for the common-translator hypothesis with Liber Proverbiorum, rhyme-royal and lexical translation practice, the mid-fifteenth-century context, uncertainty over Partenay's patron, Margaret of Anjou as a plausible rather than proven commissioner, and the ten-stanza translator epilogue.
